SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are broad—covering multiple languages and typologies, varying subword units (chars, char-trigrams, morphs, BPE), and composition functions (add, bi-LSTM, CNN)—so the core claims are well exercised. The paper includes several controlled comparisons (e.g., hand-annotated morphology vs unsupervised segmentation) and reports hyperparameters, but I see limited evidence of seed variance reporting or statistical significance testing and no mention of released code or exact preprocessing that would hinder reproducibility. Ablations on composition and unit type are present, but additional sensitivity checks (output-vocabulary size/open-vocab behavior, optimizer/scheduler effects, and more seeds) would strengthen causal claims. Overall this is a solid experimental study with minor gaps in statistical rigor and reproducibility details. | B(4): The experimental suite is broad and well-targeted to morphological questions: multiple subword units (chars, char-trigrams, Morfessor, BPE), composition functions (add, bi-LSTM, CNN), and languages spanning typologies including morphologically rich Czech and Russian with UD gold analyses. Ablations on composition functions and unit types are reported and hyperparameters are provided, and the paper explicitly tests the claim that unsupervised segmentation does not match manual morphological analyses. Limitations (fixed output vocabulary, gaps of unsupervised methods) are discussed, and related work appears thorough; however, reproductibility is weakened by no mention of released code and open-vocabulary modeling is left unaddressed, so the work is strong but missing a couple of practical/experimental completeness elements.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are broad—covering multiple languages and typologies, varying subword units (chars, char-trigrams, morphs, BPE), and composition functions (add, bi-LSTM, CNN)—so the core claims are well exercised. The paper includes several controlled comparisons (e.g., hand-annotated morphology vs unsupervised segmentation) and reports hyperparameters, but I see limited evidence of seed variance reporting or statistical significance testing and no mention of released code or exact preprocessing that would hinder reproducibility. Ablations on composition and unit type are present, but additional sensitivity checks (output-vocabulary size/open-vocab behavior, optimizer/scheduler effects, and more seeds) would strengthen causal claims. Overall this is a solid experimental study with minor gaps in statistical rigor and reproducibility details.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental suite is broad and well-targeted to morphological questions: multiple subword units (chars, char-trigrams, Morfessor, BPE), composition functions (add, bi-LSTM, CNN), and languages spanning typologies including morphologically rich Czech and Russian with UD gold analyses. Ablations on composition functions and unit types are reported and hyperparameters are provided, and the paper explicitly tests the claim that unsupervised segmentation does not match manual morphological analyses. Limitations (fixed output vocabulary, gaps of unsupervised methods) are discussed, and related work appears thorough; however, reproductibility is weakened by no mention of released code and open-vocabulary modeling is left unaddressed, so the work is strong but missing a couple of practical/experimental completeness elements.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from motivation through methods and experiments, and its main contributions are stated early (comparison of subword units, composition functions, and typology effects). Notation and methodological detail seem consistent—hyperparameters are reported and related work is thorough—supporting reproducibility-oriented readers like myself. Figures, tables, and qualitative nearest-neighbor examples are reported as effective, and overall writing quality is good, though the absence of mentioned code release and occasional density in methodological descriptions slightly hinder immediate reproducibility and accessibility. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and presents a logical flow from motivation through methods, experiments, and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and reinforced by a systematic experimental design across typologies. Notation and methodological description appear consistent with hyperparameters reported, and figures/tables (perplexity results, nearest-neighbor analyses) are informative and support claims. A few sections could use tighter signposting for readers less familiar with prior character- and morph-based work, but writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough, making the paper easy to follow for the target audience.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from motivation through methods and experiments, and its main contributions are stated early (comparison of subword units, composition functions, and typology effects). Notation and methodological detail seem consistent—hyperparameters are reported and related work is thorough—supporting reproducibility-oriented readers like myself. Figures, tables, and qualitative nearest-neighbor examples are reported as effective, and overall writing quality is good, though the absence of mentioned code release and occasional density in methodological descriptions slightly hinder immediate reproducibility and accessibility.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and presents a logical flow from motivation through methods, experiments, and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and reinforced by a systematic experimental design across typologies. Notation and methodological description appear consistent with hyperparameters reported, and figures/tables (perplexity results, nearest-neighbor analyses) are informative and support claims. A few sections could use tighter signposting for readers less familiar with prior character- and morph-based work, but writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough, making the paper easy to follow for the target audience.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against a strong set of relevant baselines (word LSTM, character bi-LSTM, character CNN, Morfessor, BPE) across multiple languages and uses the same LSTM language-model framework and perplexity metric, which supports fair controlled comparisons. It also leverages UD gold morphological annotations to test the gap with unsupervised segmenters and appears to report hyperparameters and related work thoroughly. However, the summary does not mention multiple random seeds/statistical significance, open-vocabulary baselines, or code release—all important for reproducibility—so comparisons, while broad and mostly fair, omit some robustness checks and reproducibility conveniences. | B(5): The paper evaluates a wide set of appropriate and recent baselines (word-level LSTM, char bi-LSTM, char-CNN, Morfessor, BPE) across multiple typologically diverse languages and composition functions, using the same data and perplexity metric for fair comparison. It explicitly contrasts models with gold morphological analyses and notes when unsupervised or character-based methods perform comparably or worse, and provides detailed analysis (including nearest-neighbor probes) that clarifies differences between approaches. Coverage of related work is reported as thorough and hyperparameters/datasets are controlled, supporting a comprehensive and fair empirical comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against a strong set of relevant baselines (word LSTM, character bi-LSTM, character CNN, Morfessor, BPE) across multiple languages and uses the same LSTM language-model framework and perplexity metric, which supports fair controlled comparisons. It also leverages UD gold morphological annotations to test the gap with unsupervised segmenters and appears to report hyperparameters and related work thoroughly. However, the summary does not mention multiple random seeds/statistical significance, open-vocabulary baselines, or code release—all important for reproducibility—so comparisons, while broad and mostly fair, omit some robustness checks and reproducibility conveniences.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates a wide set of appropriate and recent baselines (word-level LSTM, char bi-LSTM, char-CNN, Morfessor, BPE) across multiple typologically diverse languages and composition functions, using the same data and perplexity metric for fair comparison. It explicitly contrasts models with gold morphological analyses and notes when unsupervised or character-based methods perform comparably or worse, and provides detailed analysis (including nearest-neighbor probes) that clarifies differences between approaches. Coverage of related work is reported as thorough and hyperparameters/datasets are controlled, supporting a comprehensive and fair empirical comparison.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—subword and morphological representation for language modeling—using standard methods (LSTM LMs) and appropriate multi-language datasets and baselines, so it fits the venue audience and scope. The problem formulation and evaluation (perplexity, nearest-neighbor analyses) meet community expectations, and the systematic variation of units/composition is well aligned with venue standards. Minor concerns for a top-tier submission are the lack of mentioned code release and the fixed-output-vocabulary limitation, which reduce reproducibility and the practical impact on open-vocabulary modeling; these are resolvable and thus justify a strong but not perfect fit. | B(5): This paper is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference: it tackles a core representation question for multilingual language modeling and systematically evaluates subword units, composition functions, and typologically diverse languages (English, Turkish, Czech, Russian) using standard LSTM language models and perplexity metrics. The methodology and baselines (char-LSTM/CNN, Morfessor, BPE, word-level) and use of UD morphological annotations align with community expectations, and the finding that unsupervised segmentation lags manual morphology has clear implications for morphological modeling and low-resource work. As a researcher focused on morphology and multilingual NLP, I find the scope, datasets, and evaluations appropriate and well-positioned for the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—subword and morphological representation for language modeling—using standard methods (LSTM LMs) and appropriate multi-language datasets and baselines, so it fits the venue audience and scope. The problem formulation and evaluation (perplexity, nearest-neighbor analyses) meet community expectations, and the systematic variation of units/composition is well aligned with venue standards. Minor concerns for a top-tier submission are the lack of mentioned code release and the fixed-output-vocabulary limitation, which reduce reproducibility and the practical impact on open-vocabulary modeling; these are resolvable and thus justify a strong but not perfect fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference: it tackles a core representation question for multilingual language modeling and systematically evaluates subword units, composition functions, and typologically diverse languages (English, Turkish, Czech, Russian) using standard LSTM language models and perplexity metrics. The methodology and baselines (char-LSTM/CNN, Morfessor, BPE, word-level) and use of UD morphological annotations align with community expectations, and the finding that unsupervised segmentation lags manual morphology has clear implications for morphological modeling and low-resource work. As a researcher focused on morphology and multilingual NLP, I find the scope, datasets, and evaluations appropriate and well-positioned for the venue.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper tackles an important, practical question—how subword unit choice and composition affect language modelling across morphological typologies—and does so with a systematic experimental design (multiple languages, varied units and composition functions) that will be useful for practitioners. Its finding that character-trigram + bi-LSTM is broadly effective but still lags behind models given manual morphological analyses is a pragmatic insight that will influence modeling choices but is not paradigm-shifting. The contribution opens sensible follow-up work (better unsupervised segmentation, open-vocabulary outputs) and is likely to be built upon, but limitations (fixed output vocabulary, no mention of released code) and the incremental nature of results keep its overall impact moderate rather than transformative. | B(4): This paper tackles a central practical and scientific problem—how subword representations capture morphology across typologies—using a systematic comparison of units (chars, char-trigrams, Morfessor, BPE), composition methods, and languages, which is highly relevant for multilingual and low-resource NLP. Its finding that character-trigram embeddings composed with bi-LSTMs outperform many alternatives, and that unsupervised segmentation does not match gold morphological analyses, should change how researchers prioritize representation choices and evaluation in morphologically rich languages. The work opens clear research directions (better unsupervised morphology, composition architectures, and evaluation protocols) and is likely to be reused as a comparative benchmark, giving it strong but not purely transformative impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical question—how subword unit choice and composition affect language modelling across morphological typologies—and does so with a systematic experimental design (multiple languages, varied units and composition functions) that will be useful for practitioners. Its finding that character-trigram + bi-LSTM is broadly effective but still lags behind models given manual morphological analyses is a pragmatic insight that will influence modeling choices but is not paradigm-shifting. The contribution opens sensible follow-up work (better unsupervised segmentation, open-vocabulary outputs) and is likely to be built upon, but limitations (fixed output vocabulary, no mention of released code) and the incremental nature of results keep its overall impact moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles a central practical and scientific problem—how subword representations capture morphology across typologies—using a systematic comparison of units (chars, char-trigrams, Morfessor, BPE), composition methods, and languages, which is highly relevant for multilingual and low-resource NLP. Its finding that character-trigram embeddings composed with bi-LSTMs outperform many alternatives, and that unsupervised segmentation does not match gold morphological analyses, should change how researchers prioritize representation choices and evaluation in morphologically rich languages. The work opens clear research directions (better unsupervised morphology, composition architectures, and evaluation protocols) and is likely to be reused as a comparative benchmark, giving it strong but not purely transformative impact.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear, testable claims and evaluates them with a comprehensive set of experiments across languages, subword units, and composition functions, supporting the main empirical conclusions (e.g., character trigram + bi-LSTM outperforming many alternatives and human-annotated morphology still helping). However, the summary does not report critical reproducibility and statistical details (random seeds, variance across runs, significance testing) nor whether baselines were strictly controlled for parameter budget, which weakens confidence in some causal claims. I find no indication of mathematical derivation issues, but the lack of released code and limited discussion of open-vocabulary output are practical limitations. Overall the technical work is solid but would benefit from stronger statistical rigor and reproducibility artifacts. | B(4): The paper's primary claims are clearly stated and supported by extensive experimental evidence: cross-lingual LSTM language-model comparisons of character, char-trigram+biLSTM, char-CNN, Morfessor and BPE across typologically diverse datasets (including UD with gold morphological annotations) convincingly show the reported performance patterns. There are no mathematical proofs to evaluate, and the experimental setup uses appropriate baselines and controls (word-level, existing char models, unsupervised segmenters) with hyperparameters reported. Minor methodological concerns remain — notably the fixed-output-vocabulary constraint (acknowledged by the authors) and no mention of statistical significance testing — but these do not undermine the core empirical conclusions. Overall the technical claims are well supported, with only small gaps that merit clarification rather than invalidation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and evaluates them with a comprehensive set of experiments across languages, subword units, and composition functions, supporting the main empirical conclusions (e.g., character trigram + bi-LSTM outperforming many alternatives and human-annotated morphology still helping). However, the summary does not report critical reproducibility and statistical details (random seeds, variance across runs, significance testing) nor whether baselines were strictly controlled for parameter budget, which weakens confidence in some causal claims. I find no indication of mathematical derivation issues, but the lack of released code and limited discussion of open-vocabulary output are practical limitations. Overall the technical work is solid but would benefit from stronger statistical rigor and reproducibility artifacts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's primary claims are clearly stated and supported by extensive experimental evidence: cross-lingual LSTM language-model comparisons of character, char-trigram+biLSTM, char-CNN, Morfessor and BPE across typologically diverse datasets (including UD with gold morphological annotations) convincingly show the reported performance patterns. There are no mathematical proofs to evaluate, and the experimental setup uses appropriate baselines and controls (word-level, existing char models, unsupervised segmenters) with hyperparameters reported. Minor methodological concerns remain — notably the fixed-output-vocabulary constraint (acknowledged by the authors) and no mention of statistical significance testing — but these do not undermine the core empirical conclusions. Overall the technical claims are well supported, with only small gaps that merit clarification rather than invalidation.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper does not introduce a fundamentally new modeling paradigm but provides a systematic, cross-linguistic empirical investigation of existing subword units and composition functions (characters, char-trigrams, morphs from Morfessor/BPE, addition/bi-LSTM/CNN) in LSTM language models. Its novelty lies in the breadth and controlled comparison across morphological typologies and in the specific finding that composed character-trigram bi-LSTMs outperform most alternatives and that unsupervised segmentation trails gold morphological analyses. From an experimental-methodology perspective this is a useful and informative contribution, but methodologically it is an incremental synthesis and evaluation of known techniques rather than a non-trivial new technique. | B(3): This paper does not introduce a new modeling technique but offers a systematic, cross-typology empirical study that meaningfully clarifies how existing subword choices and composition functions interact with morphology. Comparing characters, char-trigrams, Morfessor, and BPE with multiple composition methods across several languages (including UD datasets with gold morphological analyses) is a valuable perspective that differentiates its contribution from prior single-language/method papers (e.g., Ling et al., Kim et al.). The finding that char-trigram + bi-LSTM works best and that unsupervised segmentation falls short of gold morphological analyses is informative for experts, but the work is primarily an analytical/benchmarking advance rather than a novel algorithmic contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper does not introduce a fundamentally new modeling paradigm but provides a systematic, cross-linguistic empirical investigation of existing subword units and composition functions (characters, char-trigrams, morphs from Morfessor/BPE, addition/bi-LSTM/CNN) in LSTM language models. Its novelty lies in the breadth and controlled comparison across morphological typologies and in the specific finding that composed character-trigram bi-LSTMs outperform most alternatives and that unsupervised segmentation trails gold morphological analyses. From an experimental-methodology perspective this is a useful and informative contribution, but methodologically it is an incremental synthesis and evaluation of known techniques rather than a non-trivial new technique.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper does not introduce a new modeling technique but offers a systematic, cross-typology empirical study that meaningfully clarifies how existing subword choices and composition functions interact with morphology. Comparing characters, char-trigrams, Morfessor, and BPE with multiple composition methods across several languages (including UD datasets with gold morphological analyses) is a valuable perspective that differentiates its contribution from prior single-language/method papers (e.g., Ling et al., Kim et al.). The finding that char-trigram + bi-LSTM works best and that unsupervised segmentation falls short of gold morphological analyses is informative for experts, but the work is primarily an analytical/benchmarking advance rather than a novel algorithmic contribution.

main_claims: Character representations are effective across many languages for language modeling.
A combination of character trigram representations composed with bi-LSTMs outperforms most other settings tested.
Character-level and unsupervised segmentation models do not match the predictive accuracy of models with access to explicit (hand-annotated) morphological analyses.
The study systematically varies subword units, composition functions, and morphological typology to analyze their interactions.
_summary.methodology: Train LSTM language models that compute word representations by composing learned subword-unit embeddings (characters, character trigrams, or morphs from Morfessor/BPE) with different composition functions (addition, bi-LSTM, CNN), and compare model performance (perplexity and qualitative nearest-neighbor analyses) across multiple languages and morphological typologies.
datasets: Datasets from Ling et al. (2015) for English and Turkish
Universal Dependencies (UD) datasets with human-annotated morphological analyses for Czech and Russian
Processed Wikipedia data (Al-Rfou et al., 2013) ~1.2M train tokens and ~150K dev/test tokens per dataset
metrics: Cosine similarity for nearest-neighbor qualitative analysis
limitations: Models can predict only words from a fixed output vocabulary, so open-vocabulary prediction is not addressed.
Unsupervised morphological segmentation is an inadequate substitute for manual morphological analysis in terms of predictive accuracy.
Character models do not fully capture morphology compared to models with access to explicit morphological analyses.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - subword/morphological representation for language modeling

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and reproducibility lead with extensive experience designing and auditing empirical NLP experiments for language modeling and representation learning.
traits: insists on thorough ablation studies and controlled comparisons (e.g., same parameter budgets, training regimes) to support causal claims
checks for statistical rigor: variance across seeds, significance testing, and robustness to dataset/sample size
prioritizes reproducibility: clear hyperparameter lists, training curves, code/data release, and exact preprocessing details
examines implementation pitfalls: optimizer/scheduler choices, embedding parameter counts, and fair baseline implementations
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments rigorously support the paper's claims and whether results can be reliably reproduced and interpreted.

reviewer_b.name: Morphology & NLP Impact
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in computational morphology and multilingual NLP, with a track record of evaluating linguistic representations across typologically diverse languages and deploying models in real-world multilingual settings.
traits: evaluates novelty and positioning relative to prior work on subword/morphological representations and recent unsupervised segmentation methods
assesses linguistic relevance: whether the experiments meaningfully probe morphology across typologies and reveal actionable insights for modeling languages with rich morphology
values broader impact and applicability: implications for downstream tasks, low-resource languages, and toolkit adoption
looks for insightful qualitative analyses (e.g., error modes, nearest-neighbor examples) that connect model behavior to linguistic phenomena
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper by its contribution to understanding morphology-aware representations in NLP and the significance of its findings for future research and applications.
